_note: Worked example — FICTIONAL scenario (no real people, projects, or employers). Shows the register and labeling depth to copy. Your real entries are gitignored by default; write decision.context BEFORE re-reading how things ended.

decision.title: Rewrite the notification service vs incremental strangler-fig refactor
decision.context: Team of three owns an aging Node notification service: duplicated retry logic across four code paths, worst on-call burden in the org, and a committed Q3 feature that depends on it. The senior engineer who owns the legacy retry logic has flagged burnout and dropped to reduced availability. A full rewrite has been proposed twice before and rejected for risk.
decision.rationale: Chose the strangler-fig incremental path. A full rewrite risks the Q3 commitment and repeats the pattern of big-bang proposals dying mid-flight; deferring entirely leaves the on-call burden compounding. Incremental migration keeps shipping while capping downside — if it stalls, we still have a working (if ugly) service, whereas a stalled rewrite leaves us maintaining two.
options_considered: Full rewrite on the new platform stack
Strangler-fig incremental migration, top paths first
Defer: contractor patches the worst retry bugs, revisit next year
decision.chosen_option: Strangler-fig incremental migration, top paths first

forecasts.question: Migration of the top three notification paths is complete within 90 days
On-call pages attributed to the service drop by half within the quarter
We end up doing a full rewrite anyway within 12 months

outcome.summary: Migration finished at 130 days, not 90 — the senior engineer left at week six and the undocumented legacy retry behavior took weeks to reverse-engineer. Pages did drop by more than half. No rewrite followed.

failures.description: Senior engineer departed at week six, taking undocumented knowledge of the legacy retry logic; the burnout signal was visible at decision time
Dual-write phase ran twice as long as planned, roughly doubling the service's infra cost for a quarter

score_rationales.risk_comprehensiveness: Rationale engages schedule risk and the stalled-rewrite failure mode, but never names the bus-factor risk despite the burnout signal being in the record, and dual-write cost goes unmentioned: 3.
score_rationales.calibration_given_knowable: 0.75 / 0.6 / 0.2 are differentiated and track the stated evidence (committed deadline, known pain, prior rewrite rejections); no base-rate reasoning about migration slippage, so 4 not 5.
score_rationales.process_quality: Three real options with explicit tradeoffs and a reasoned downside cap (stalled-incremental beats stalled-rewrite); no disconfirming step and no kill criterion for abandoning the migration: 4.

expected_premortem_risks: The senior owner of the legacy retry logic becomes unavailable mid-migration (burnout signal already visible)
Dual-write phase runs long, doubling infra cost and eroding stakeholder patience
Hidden coupling in the legacy retry paths makes 'top three paths' larger than scoped
Q3 feature work and migration compete for the same three people
The 'pages drop by half' target gets gamed by silencing alerts rather than fixing causes
